topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking me with its finality. Liam’s handwriting, usually a cascade of enthusiastic scribbles, was neat, almost clinical. I picked it up, my thumb tracing the embossed seal, and the air in the apartment suddenly felt thick, like wading through syrup. He was moving. Across the country. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I didn't move. I just stared at the peeling paint on the wall, the familiar pattern suddenly seeming alien and sharp. When he finally walked in, whistling that infuriatingly cheerful tune, I managed a tight, brittle smile. "So," I said, my voice sounding unnaturally level, "that’s… a big change." I didn't look up as he started unloading boxes, the sheer volume of his belongings a physical weight pressing down on the room.

[story 2]

I reread the email for the tenth time. The details swam before my eyes: new zip codes, lease agreements, a timeline that made no sense. A slow burn started in my chest, a simmering heat that refused to dissipate. I spent the afternoon pacing the length of my living room, my shoes scuffing the hardwood floor with rhythmic, angry taps. Every time I stopped, I’d run a hand through my hair, feeling the tension coil tighter around my neck. When Maya called, her voice bright and full of plans for the weekend, I forced a laugh that felt like cracking plaster. "That sounds… exciting, Maya," I managed, my tone clipped. I kept focusing on the mundane—making coffee, straightening a crooked picture frame—anything but the seismic shift happening in my own equilibrium.

[story 3]

The news hit me during a late-night drive home. The landscape blurred past in a smear of neon and dark highway, but the core of the information—Liam leaving—was crystal clear. I gripped the steering wheel so hard my knuckles were white islands against the dark plastic. A deep, internal resistance set in, a stubborn refusal to acknowledge the reality. I didn't call him. I didn't text. I just drove, the engine a low, monotonous hum beneath a rising tide of frustration. I kept picturing his face, expecting some logical explanation to surface, but all that emerged was a stubborn refusal to let the feeling settle, to allow the quiet acknowledgment of how much this separation would cost. I just kept pushing the accelerator, determined to keep moving forward, even though every fiber of my being wanted to stop dead in my tracks.
